preamble.summary: Psalm 8 is a hymn of praise to the Creator, framed by the refrain 'O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is Your name in all the earth.' Between the bookends of cosmic praise, the psalmist gazes at the night sky, marvels that God attends to human beings at all, and declares that God has crowned humanity with glory and honor, placing all creation under human dominion. It is the Psalter's creation theology in miniature.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm asks the question that no other creature can ask: 'What is a human being that You are mindful of him?' (mah enosh ki tizkrennu). The question emerges from looking at the stars — the vastness of the cosmos provokes not pride but astonishment at divine attention. The answer is equally astonishing: God has made humanity 'a little lower than the heavenly beings' (me'at from Elohim) and crowned them with kavod ve-hadar ('glory and honor'). The same kavod that belongs to God is shared with human beings. Humanity's place is not at the bottom of creation (mere dust) or at the top (divine), but in the extraordinary middle — a little less than God, ruling over everything else. The psalm is simultaneously the highest anthropology and the deepest humility in the Hebrew Bible.
preamble.friction: The phrase me'at from Elohim (v. 6, Heb.) is famously ambiguous. Elohim can mean 'God' or 'gods' or 'heavenly beings' or 'angels.' The Septuagint chose 'angels' (angelous), and Hebrews 2:7 follows that rendering. If it means 'God,' the claim is staggering: humanity is just below God Himself. If it means 'angels,' the claim is still remarkable but slightly lower on the cosmic hierarchy. The psalm's vision of human dominion over creation has been critiqued as licensing environmental exploitation, but the Hebrew verb mashal ('to rule') implies responsible stewardship under God, not autonomous exploitation — the human rules as God's deputy, not as God's replacement.
preamble.connections: The psalm echoes Genesis 1:26-28 (dominion over animals, image of God). The list of creatures under human rule (v. 8-9) follows the same categories as Genesis 1: livestock, wild animals, birds, fish. Hebrews 2:6-8 quotes this psalm and applies the 'son of man' language to Jesus. 1 Corinthians 15:27 uses the 'all things under his feet' language for Christ's final reign. The refrain structure (identical opening and closing) is unique in the Psalter's first book.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Gittith, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. According to the Gittith.
A psalm of David.
translator_notes: Gittith may refer to a musical instrument from Gath (a Philistine city), a winepress song (gat means 'winepress'), or a melody associated with the grape harvest. The term appears in the superscriptions of Psalms 8, 81, and 84. If it refers to a harvest melody, the creation-celebration content of Psalm 8 would suit a festive, agricultural setting.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֤ה אֲדֹנֵ֗ינוּ מָֽה־אַדִּ֣יר שִׁ֭מְךָ בְּכׇל־הָאָ֑רֶץ אֲשֶׁ֥ר תְּנָ֥ה ה֝וֹדְךָ֗ עַל־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD our Lord, how excellent is thy name in all the earth! who hast set thy glory above the heavens.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, our Lord,
how majestic is Your name in all the earth —
You who have set Your splendor above the heavens!
translator_notes: The distinction between YHWH (the personal covenant name) and Adonenu ('our Lord, our master') is important. YHWH is who God is; Adon is what God is to us. The combination declares both identity and relationship. The plural possessive -enu ('our') makes this a communal confession, not merely individual praise.

key_terms.semantic_range: majestic, mighty, noble, glorious, powerful, magnificent
key_terms.note: addir describes overwhelming impressiveness — the word is used for mighty waters (Psalm 93:4), noble rulers (Judges 5:13), and towering trees (Ezekiel 17:23). Applied to God's name, it means God's revealed identity is the most impressive reality in all the earth.

verses.text_hebrew: מִפִּ֤י עֽוֹלְלִ֨ים ׀ וְֽיֹנְקִים֮ יִסַּ֢דְתָּ֫ עֹ֥ז לְמַ֥עַן צוֹרְרֶ֑יךָ לְהַשְׁבִּ֥ית א֝וֹיֵ֗ב וּמִתְנַקֵּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Out of the mouth of babes and sucklings hast thou ordained strength because of thine enemies, that thou mightest still the enemy and the avenger.
verses.rendering:
From the mouths of infants and nursing babies
You have established strength
because of Your adversaries,
to silence the enemy and the avenger.
verses.expanded_rendering: One of the most mysterious verses in the Psalter. Mi-pi olelim ve-yonqim ('from the mouth of infants and nursing babies') — olelim are toddlers, barely verbal; yonqim are nursing infants, pre-verbal. Yet from their mouths yissadta oz ('You have established strength, You have founded a stronghold'). The verb yasad means 'to found, to establish, to lay a foundation' — God builds a fortress of strength from the mouths of those who cannot yet speak coherently. The purpose: le-ma'an tsorerekha ('because of Your adversaries, on account of those who press against You') — to silence the oyev u-mitnaqem ('the enemy and the avenger'). The paradox is deliberate: the weakest voices undo the strongest opposition. Infants confound enemies. This is the Psalter's theology of divine reversal — God's power works through what the world considers powerless.
translator_notes: Jesus quotes this verse in Matthew 21:16 when children cry 'Hosanna to the Son of David' in the temple and the chief priests object. Jesus reads the psalm as fulfilled in the children's praise. The Septuagint translates oz ('strength') as ainon ('praise'), shifting the meaning from 'You have established strength' to 'You have perfected praise' — both readings are theologically productive.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אֶרְאֶ֣ה שָׁ֭מֶיךָ מַעֲשֵׂ֣ה אֶצְבְּעֹתֶ֑יךָ יָרֵ֥חַ וְ֝כוֹכָבִ֗ים אֲשֶׁ֣ר כּוֹנָֽנְתָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: When I consider thy heavens, the work of thy fingers, the moon and the stars, which thou hast ordained;
verses.rendering:
When I look at Your heavens,
the work of Your fingers,
the moon and the stars that You have set in place —
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist gazes upward and the great question of the psalm emerges from what he sees. Ki er'eh shamekha ('when I see Your heavens') — the heavens are shamekha ('Your heavens'), God's personal handiwork. Ma'aseh etsbe'otekha ('the work of Your fingers') is an intimate anthropomorphism — not God's hands (power) but God's fingers (precision, artistry, delicacy). The heavens are finger-work, detailed craftsmanship. Yareach ve-khokhavim ('the moon and the stars') specify what the psalmist sees: this is a nighttime psalm. The sun is not mentioned — the observation happens under a star-filled sky, which is arguably more awe-inducing than daylight because it reveals the depth of the cosmos. Asher konantah ('which You have established, set in place') — the verb kun means to fix firmly, to establish securely. The stars are not scattered randomly; they are placed.
translator_notes: The nighttime setting (moon and stars, no sun) is significant. The night sky reveals more of the universe than daylight does — the naked eye can see thousands of stars, each one placed by God's fingers. The experience of smallness under the stars is universal, and the psalm captures it precisely: the vastness of the cosmos provokes the question of human significance.

verses.text_kjv: What is man, that thou art mindful of him? and the son of man, that thou visitest him?
verses.rendering:
What is a human being that You remember him,
a son of Adam that You attend to him?
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's central question — and one of the most important questions in the Hebrew Bible. Mah enosh ki tizkrennu ('what is a mortal that You remember him?'). The word enosh (rather than adam or ish) emphasizes human frailty — enosh derives from anash ('to be weak, to be sick, to be mortal'). This is not 'what is humanity in its grandeur?' but 'what is this weak, mortal creature?' The parallel u-ven adam ki tifqedennu ('and the son of Adam that You visit him, attend to him') uses ben adam ('son of Adam, human being') — emphasizing the earthly origin (adam from adamah, 'ground'). The verb paqad ('to visit, to attend to, to number, to reckon with') implies purposeful engagement — God does not merely notice humanity but intervenes in human affairs. The question is not rhetorical despair but astonished wonder: given the scale of the cosmos, why would the God who made the stars bother with creatures made from dust?
translator_notes: The term ben adam ('son of man, son of Adam') here means simply 'human being.' In Daniel 7:13, the phrase takes on a more exalted meaning ('one like a son of man' approaching God's throne), and in the Gospels, Jesus adopts it as his primary self-designation. The trajectory from Psalm 8's humble 'mortal creature' to Daniel's heavenly figure to Jesus' self-identification is one of the most significant theological developments in biblical tradition.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתְּחַסְּרֵ֣הוּ מְ֭עַט מֵאֱלֹהִ֑ים וְכָב֖וֹד וְהָדָ֣ר תְּעַטְּרֵֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou hast made him a little lower than the angels, and hast crowned him with glory and honour.
verses.rendering:
Yet You made him a little lower than God,
and You crown him with glory and honor.
verses.expanded_rendering: The answer to 'what is a human being?' is staggering. Va-techasrehu me'at me-Elohim ('and You made him lack a little from Elohim'). The verb chasar means 'to lack, to be less than, to be diminished.' Me'at ('a little, slightly') limits the gap. The question is: what does Elohim mean here? If 'God' — humanity is positioned just below the Creator Himself. If 'heavenly beings' or 'angels' (as the Septuagint reads) — humanity sits just below the divine council. Either reading places humanity astonishingly high on the scale of being. The second line delivers the coronation: ve-khavod ve-hadar te'atterehu ('and with glory and honor You crown him'). Kavod ('glory, weight, significance') and hadar ('honor, splendor, majesty') are royal terms — words used for God's own majesty (Psalm 96:6, 104:1). God shares His own royal attributes with human beings. The verb atar ('to crown, to surround') from Psalm 5:13 appears again — humanity is crowned the way a king is crowned, invested with dignity that comes from above.
translator_notes: The Septuagint's translation of Elohim as angelous ('angels') influenced the New Testament quotation in Hebrews 2:7. The Hebrew more naturally reads 'God' (Elohim without qualification normally means God), making the anthropological claim even bolder: humanity was made just slightly less than divine. The psalm does not explain how this is possible — it simply declares it with wonder.

Kavod ('glory') here echoes its use throughout the Psalter for God's own splendor. When the psalm says God crowns humanity with kavod, it is saying that the same weight, significance, and radiance that characterize God are shared — in diminished but real measure — with human beings. This is the Hebrew Bible's version of the imago Dei ('image of God') from Genesis 1:26-27.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, significance, splendor, dignity; the visible manifestation of worth
key_terms.note: kavod derives from kaved ('to be heavy, to be weighty'). Glory in Hebrew is not ethereal but substantial — it has weight and presence. God's kavod fills the temple (1 Kings 8:11), appears on Sinai (Exodus 24:16-17), and defines the divine nature. When humanity receives kavod, it receives a share in God's own substantial dignity.

key_terms.semantic_range: honor, splendor, majesty, grandeur, beauty; the outward display of inherent worth
key_terms.note: hadar describes visible magnificence — the majestic appearance of a king, the splendor of God's works, the beauty of old age (Proverbs 20:29). Paired with kavod, it creates a complete picture of invested dignity: kavod is the inner weight; hadar is the outer display.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּ֭מְשִׁילֵהוּ בְּמַעֲשֵׂ֣י יָדֶ֑יךָ כֹּ֝֗ל שַׁ֣תָּה תַֽחַת־רַגְלָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou madest him to have dominion over the works of thy hands; thou hast put all things under his feet:
verses.rendering:
You give him dominion over the works of Your hands;
You have placed everything under his feet:
translator_notes: 1 Corinthians 15:27 applies this verse to Christ: 'For God has put all things under his feet.' The move from humanity-in-general (Psalm 8) to Christ-in-particular (Paul) reflects early Christian theology's reading of the psalm as ultimately fulfilled in Jesus, the ideal human being who exercises the dominion that Adam forfeited.

verses.text_hebrew: צֹנֶ֣ה וַאֲלָפִ֣ים כֻּלָּ֑ם וְ֝גַ֗ם בַּהֲמ֥וֹת שָׂדָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: All sheep and oxen, yea, and the beasts of the field;
verses.rendering:
sheep and cattle, all of them,
and also the wild animals of the field,
translator_notes: The list of creatures under human dominion follows the Genesis 1 categories. Tsoneh ('sheep, flocks') and alafim ('cattle, oxen') represent domesticated animals. Bahamot sadai ('beasts of the field') represent wild land animals. The progression from domestic to wild reflects increasing degrees of human authority — ruling tame animals is easy; ruling wild ones demonstrates the full scope of the mandate.

verses.text_hebrew: צִפּ֣וֹר שָׁ֭מַיִם וּדְגֵ֣י הַיָּ֑ם עֹ֝בֵ֗ר אׇרְח֥וֹת יַמִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The fowl of the air, and the fish of the sea, and whatsoever passeth through the paths of the seas.
verses.rendering:
the birds of the sky and the fish of the sea —
whatever passes along the paths of the seas.
verses.expanded_rendering: The dominion extends to sky and sea. Tsippor shamayim ('birds of the heavens') — creatures above human reach, flying in God's own domain. U-dge hayyam ('and fish of the sea') — creatures beneath human sight, moving in the depths. Over archot yammim ('the paths of the seas') — even the sea lanes, the routes taken by creatures in the deep ocean, fall under human authority. The comprehensiveness is total: land (v. 8), sky, and sea (v. 9) — the three domains of Genesis 1's creation. Nothing in the animal world falls outside the mandate. Yet this authority remains delegated: it is God who placed (shattah, v. 7) these things under human feet. Remove God from the equation, and the mandate has no foundation.
translator_notes: The phrase archot yammim ('paths of the seas') is poetically evocative — it suggests that the sea has invisible routes, like roads, traveled by its creatures. Matthew Fontaine Maury, the 19th-century oceanographer considered the father of modern oceanography, reportedly was inspired by this phrase to search for ocean currents, though the historical details of that account are debated.

verses.text_kjv: O LORD our Lord, how excellent is thy name in all the earth!
verses.rendering:
O LORD, our Lord,
how majestic is Your name in all the earth!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes where it began — an exact repetition of the opening refrain. The inclusio (literary bracket) is complete: YHWH Adonenu mah addir shimkha be-khol ha-arets. But the repetition carries new weight. Before the psalm's interior, the refrain was pure praise. After gazing at the stars, asking about human significance, discovering humanity's coronation with glory, and surveying the scope of human dominion, the same words now carry the freight of everything between them. The majestic name is majestic not only because of stars and heavens but because this God attended to human beings, crowned them, and entrusted creation to them. The praise has deepened through understanding. The refrain is the same; the worshiper is changed.
translator_notes: The exact repetition of the refrain with no modification is a deliberate structural choice. It signals that the psalm's content has not altered the fundamental truth — God's name remains majestic in all the earth — but has expanded the worshiper's comprehension of what that majesty includes. The frame holds; the picture inside it is now richer.
